title: DAVID — Cnut the Great: The King the Skalds Praised
logline: Biography of Cnut the Great with attested Old Norse skaldic court poetry composed for him; sources on file.

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Royal Tongues — dead languages and the voices of history
brand.cta: Follow the Archive — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

sources.citation: Óttarr svarti, Knútsdrápa (c. 1026) — skaldic praise-poem composed for Cnut, preserved in the kings' sagas
sources.notes: Court skaldic verse contemporary with Cnut; period Old Norse register, not Cnut's own composition

sources.citation: Anglo-Saxon Chronicle (11th c.) — Cnut's conquest of England, reign, and pilgrimage to Rome
sources.notes: West Saxon vernacular annals of the conquest and reign

sources.citation: Encomium Emmae Reginae (c. 1041–1042) — Latin panegyric on Cnut's queen and the Danish dynasty
sources.notes: Contemporary Latin court source for the North Sea empire

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Tonight, Cnut the Great — a Danish prince who became king of England, Denmark, and Norway, and ruled an empire that spanned the North Sea.
Born around nine ninety-five, he conquered England by twenty-eighteen, then inherited and won two more crowns. Later legend has him commanding the tide — to prove to flatterers that no king could.
His was a three-language court: Old Norse among his Danish warriors and poets, Old English for governing his richest kingdom, and Latin for the church he paid to keep.
Knútr inn ríki — Cnut the Mighty — the name his Norse skalds gave him in the praise-poems they performed in his own hall, dense with kennings for a gold-giving sea-king.
beats.on_screen: ATTESTED · Old Norse · skaldic court poetry

beats.speech_text: He died at Shaftesbury in ten thirty-five, and his North Sea empire died soon after him. What survives is the skalds' verse — a king remembered in the meter of the men who served him.

lines: PERIOD LINE: Attested Old Norse skaldic epithet/register — court poetry about Cnut, not his own words
METHOD: Skaldic corpus (Óttarr svarti) + Anglo-Saxon Chronicle + Encomium Emmae
